headline: Nickolas Asset Management Buys Resin Processing Facility From Material Difference Technologies
summary_text: Nickolas Asset Management Buys Resin Processing Facility From Material Difference Technologies # Nickolas Asset Management Buys Resin Processing Facility From Material Difference Technologies By Amit Chowdhry • Feb 14, 2026 Nickolas Asset Management Buys Resin Processing Facility From Material Difference Technologies Nickolas Asset Management (NAM) has acquired a resin processing and materials facility from Material Difference Technologies (MDT), a Florida-based supplier and processor of plastic resins serving industrial and consumer manufacturing markets. The deal adds a 120,000-square-foot operation with the ability to process millions of pounds of reprocessed plastic resins annually, expanding NAM’s materials platform and reinforcing the firm’s emphasis on asset- and infrastructure-heavy manufacturing. The acquired site combines resin distribution with formulating, blending, and compounding capabilities that NAM said will support its broader manufacturing platform with tailored material solutions aligned to specific product, performance, and customer requirements. In addition to production capacity, the facility includes technical development support, quality assurance, and in-house analytical laboratory services. NAM positioned the deal as a step toward deeper vertical integration, aiming to increase control over materials, quality, cost, and supply chain reliability across the businesses it owns and operates. By bringing materials processing closer to finished-product manufacturing, the firm expects to improve consistency and responsiveness while ... The facility is dedicated to recycling and reprocessing post-consumer and post-industrial plastic scrap into reusable resin pellets, a process NAM said diverts tens of millions of pounds of plastic annually from landfills. NAM added that the recycled resin product lines produced at the site will hold multiple third-party certifications, including SCS Global Services and the Association of Plastic Recyclers’ PCR Certification Program, which validate responsible recycling practices and quality standards. NAM said the acquisition was led internally by CEO Brian Nichols, CFO Dan McMachen, and COO Joe Klein, who oversaw ... relationships, and operations. NAM described itself as an evergreen, operator-led family office built on permanent capital and a long-term ownership philosophy. Following the acquisition, the firm said it maintains a national footprint of eight manufacturing facilities totaling approximately 2.5 million square feet of production space across the United States. headline: Nickolas Asset Management Completes Acquisition of Plastic Resin Processing Facility
summary_text: Nickolas Asset Management Completes Acquisition of Plastic Resin Processing Facility Nickolas Asset Management Nickolas Asset Management(“NAM”), an operator-led investment firm with deep experience in industrial manufacturing and the plastics sector, has completed the acquisition of a 120,000-square-foot plastic resin processing and materials facility from Material Difference Technologies (“MDT”), a Florida-based supplier and processor of plastic resins serving diverse industrial and consumer manufacturing markets. The acquired facility is dedicated to the recycling and reprocessing of post-consumer and post-industrial plastic scrap into reusable resin pellets, thereby diverting tens of millions of pounds of plastic from landfills annually. It combines distribution with formulating, blending, and compounding capabilities, and includes technical development, quality assurance, and in-house analytical laboratory services that support NAM’s vertically integrated manufacturing platform and contractual compliance with third-party certifications, including the Association of Plastic Recyclers (APR) PCR Certification Program. This transaction reinforces NAM’s strategic objective of strengthening control over plastic material supply, quality standards, cost structures, and supply chain reliability in its manufacturing businesses. By bringing plastic materials processing closer to finished product manufacturing, NAM seeks to improve operational consistency, responsiveness to contractual obligations, and long-term competitiveness under evolving regulatory and sustainability frameworks. Under the terms of the acquisition, the continuing operational leadership of the facility will be maintained by the former owner, who will remain as Operating Partner and Chief Operating Officer of the division to ensure continuity of customer relationships and technical expertise. NAM operates as an active, operator-led partner and maintains a national footprint of eight manufacturing facilities totaling approximately 2.5 million square feet of production space across the United States. summary_text: NOVA Chemicals Launches Recycled Stretch Film Resins - Skip to primary sidebar - Skip to main content - Skip to primary navigation NOVA Chemicals has commercialized two new mechanically recycled polyethylene grades under its SYNDIGO portfolio, expanding options for non-food flexible packaging applications across North America. The new grades, SYNDIGO rPE-IN3 and SYNDIGO rPE-IN4, are produced at the company’s SYNDIGO1 mechanical recycling facility in Connersville, Indiana. Both are recycled linear low-density polyethylene resins made from 100 percent post-consumer recycled film. That capacity positions the site as one of the larger dedicated film-to-film mechanical recycling operations in North America. ## Expanding the SYNDIGO Portfolio The broader SYNDIGO portfolio already includes: • A white recycled LLDPE resin for protective packaging and carry-out bags• A recycled HDPE resin for both food and non-food contact applications Later in 2026, NOVA expects to introduce a 100 percent recycled LLDPE suitable for food-contact applications. summary_text: ICIS: Private equity tightens grip on Brazil’s rPET sector Advertisement - Private equity embraces long‑horizon, higher‑risk assets - Near‑term softness masks improving long‑range outlook - Global petrochemical weakness accelerates divestments A wave of recent exits of recycling assets — including shutdowns across multiple regions — highlights a widening divergence in investment strategy. The pattern mirrors broader petrochemicals, where prolonged weakness is driving listed producers to divest non‑core, low‑return assets, while private equity steps in to acquire or scale platforms that can be optimized for eventual exit. Thailand‑listed Indorama Ventures shuttered its rPET facility in Minas Gerais, Brazil, in September 2025 and has since divested an additional rPET asset in the Czech Republic. Mexico‑listed Alpek also announced the closure of its rPET plant in Pennsylvania, US, by March, after shutting down its Cedar Creek site in North Carolina in July 2025. headline: After months of stability, polyethylene prices move higher
summary_text: Prices for polyethylene resins in North America are up an average of 5 cents per pound since Jan. 1, according to market sources. The increase resulted from stronger demand combined with some production issues after recent cold weather on the Gulf Coast. The last notable change occurred in June, when prices rose by 3 cents per pound, reversing 3 cent decline the prior month. Since then, the market had remained largely flat.

headline: Invista partners with Epoch Biodesign to chemically recycle nylon 66
summary_text: Together, Invista and Epoch Biodesign plan to produce customer-validated, virgin-quality recycled nylon 66 at commercial scale. Under the agreement, Epoch's enzymes will be used to break down nylon waste into its original monomers, which can then be repolymerised by Invista into new nylon 66. Technical assessments and polymer qualification are already underway, with application performance testing planned as the next phase. " When your recycling technology is chosen from a global set of options by a company whose heritage is directly linked to the invention of nylon 66, it is clearly a defining moment ," said Epoch Biodesign CEO Jacob Nathan. " We are excited to increase momentum towards our goal of global deployment; this is a clear message that one of the largest producers of nylon 66 believes in our technology and the need for effective, enzymatic recycling at commercial scale.

That's according to a newly filed deed. The nonprofit group's announcement that it bought Milwaukee's oldest cinema didn't include a purchase price. The theater, 2589 N. Downer Ave., was sold by Seattle-based B33 Downer Avenue LLC to IFM Downer LLC, a Milwaukee Film affiliate, according to a deed posted online by the Wisconsin Department of Revenue on March 4. The two-screen theater was sold as a newly formed commercial condominium unit, according to the deed. That condo declaration allowed the seller, an affiliate of Bridge 33 Capital, to break off the cinema from the larger ... The nonprofit behind the Milwaukee Film Festival – and operator of the Oriental Theatre since 2018 – has purchased the 111-year-old two-screen Downer Theatre, Milwaukee Film officials announced March 2. “Owning the Downer Theatre is a dream-come-true for Milwaukee Film,” Milwaukee Film Executive Director Susan Kerns said in a news release. “It affords our organization stronger financial footing and ensures our ability to provide the city we love with the film festivals and year-round programming it deserves. We’re delighted to close this deal in time for ... Bridge33 Capital purchased the Downer Theatre, 2589 N. Downer Ave., and surrounding businesses in early 2020. Milwaukee Film operated the Downer beginning in 2024 through a rental agreement with Bridge33, with Milwaukee Film reporting an ... United study. Several private donations and a low-interest loan courtesy of The Helmerich Trust helped make Milwaukee Film's Downer ... a sustainable, long-term owner," Matt Galas, asset manager at Bridge33 Capital, said in the release. "By revitalizing ... “Buying the building is just the beginning,” Kerns said in the release, of acquiring the Downer. “We have ambitious plans to restore this historic cinema in a way that honors its legacy while ensuring it serves today’s audiences. Our vision includes upgraded seating, expanded accessibility features, upgraded audio and projection systems, and the addition of 35mm film capabilities to reconnect the theater with its rich tradition of cinema exhibition. We are also ... Milwaukee Film has endured some financial struggles in recent years, but its picture has improved. In 2024, it reported revenue of $5.38 million and expenses of $5.75 million, with a loss of $367,687. That's according to ProPublica Nonprofit Explorer, which compiles annual reports filed with the Internal Revenue Service. Milwaukee Film in 2023 lost $2.5 million after recording revenue of just under $5 million and expenses of nearly $7.5 ... Jonathan Jackson, Milwaukee Film CEO for nearly 16 years, resigned in early 2024. Anne Reed, who had retired as head of the Wisconsin Humane Society, became Milwaukee Film's interim CEO. In August 2024, the organization cut education and artist services programs and several positions to focus on its two theaters, the annual film festival and "financial stability," Reed told the Journal Sentinel at the time. (Courtesy photo) Shirley Sneve ICT FARGO — The Northern Plains Indigenous Film Festival debuts April 17 at the historic Fargo Theatre in Fargo, North Dakota, establishing a new home for Indigenous cinema in the Northern Plains and greater Midwest. Founded to create a platform for Indigenous storytelling through film, the festival centers contemporary Indigenous stories while bringing filmmakers, audiences, and creative professionals into the same room. The two-day event will feature Indigenous-made narrative, documentary, and short films, filmmaker conversations, and programming designed to ... While Indigenous film festivals exist across the country, few are rooted in the Northern Plains. The festival was created to fill that gap, offering a space where Indigenous filmmakers can share work grounded in the cultures, histories, and lived experiences of this region while also welcoming national and international perspectives. “Film is one of the most powerful tools Indigenous people have for telling our own stories,” said Joseph Williams, Sisseton Wahpeton Oyate, co-founder and festival co- director. “This festival is about visibility, access, and building ... Anna Johnson, Turtle Mountain Band of Chippewa and also festival co-founder and co-director, said it was a natural fit for the event to be held in Fargo. “Native art is part of Fargo’s identity,” she said. “We aren’t a subcategory. We’re ... Screenings and filmmaker panel discussions will be held at the Fargo Theatre while additional programming will be held at North Dakota State University’s Barry Hall including filmmaker workshops led by Ben West and Yancey Burns of Rena Flying Coyote Collective, Jennifer Martell, and a presentation by Liza Black, author of Picturing Indians, focusing on ... Film submissions are being accepted through March 30. More information on the festival can be found at northernplainsfestival.com. headline: Iowa revives film rebate program years after scandal, but independent filmmakers say it falls short
summary_text: Iowa revives film rebate program years after scandal, but independent filmmakers say it falls short CEDAR RAPIDS, Iowa (KCRG) - Iowa is launching a new film rebate program nearly two decades after its last incentive program ended in scandal. The two-year pilot program offers up to 30% back on qualifying expenses. To qualify, productions must come from Iowa studios, carry a budget of at least $1 million, film mostly in the state, and be made available to the public. The Iowa Economic Development Authority said the Renovo Media Group in Clear Lake, Iowa proposed the concept for the new rebate program. It is Iowa’s first major rebate program since the state abruptly ended its previous incentive program in 2009 after millions of dollars were misused. The state said the new program has more safeguards against fraud, including specifying allowable and non-allowable expenses. It also requires a production’s final budget be reviewed by a third-party before approval. Eric Dean Freese, director of the Cedar Rapids Independent Film Festival, said the program represents progress but leaves out Iowa’s independent filmmakers. “It’s baby steps,” Freese said. He said the new program is not competitive enough with other states. “I’m not really satisfied with this piece of legislation,” Freese said. “I think a better program would be one that appreciates the work that’s being done by those smaller budget productions.” The new rebate program comes as the state recently put its Greenlight Grant for low-budget films on pause — a grant Freese said many filmmakers relied on. The state said it will be evaluating the Greenlight Grant program “to ensure it has met the goals to advance Iowa’s ... Filmmaker Brittany Benedict directed ’ 5683,’ a romantic comedy filmed in Eastern Iowa with a local crew. ... “If you don’t know, that really is not a lot to make a feature film,” Benedict said. “So, we kind of went back to our ... “Maybe just something more on, like, a lower scale, just to really see that there is a lot of talented individuals out ... Actress Caitlin Allen grew up in Cedar Rapids and said it was exciting to film in her hometown. She said the ... “It’s a smaller market, but that also allows for really amazing talent to shine through,” Allen said. “I think that that’s really important, especially if you’re going to end up growing your career.” The film is streaming on Amazon Prime. Freese said the film represents Iowa’s growing industry. He said filmmakers just need more resources. “We’re missing out on an opportunity to create jobs, to create work, and to create films that are a calling card to the type of talent and type of work that’s being done in Iowa,” Freese said. The application window for the pilot rebate program is open through March 16, 2026. Funding runs through 2027. In a statement to TV9, the Iowa Economic Development Authority said it hopes the program will support large-scale productions in the state. So far, it says it’s received four applications for the rebate program. #### Most Read #### Latest News

headline: Missouri’s Film Tax Credits Deliver Big Return as Productions Surge Statewide - Missourinet
summary_text: Missouri’s Film Tax Credits Deliver Big Return as Productions Surge Statewide - Missourinet -- # Missouri’s Film Tax Credits Deliver Big Return as Productions Surge Statewide Photo Credit: Visit Hermann, A Christmas Vintage Missouri’s film industry had a big year in 2025, with movie and TV crews spending more than $40 million while shooting around the state. Missouri Film Office director Andrea Sporcic Klund, told Missourinet that it’s a great number. Andrea Sporcic Klund, Missouri Film Office Director. Photo courtesy and provided by Andrea Sporcic Klund. “I think in relation to how much tax credit was allocated for that 40 million only 15.7 million,” said Klund. “So that ... Klund said those projects ranged from feature films and documentaries to two dozen commercials shot across the state. She said the ease of filming keeps film crews coming back to the Show-Me State. “They’re able to hire the crews,” said Klund. “The support companies are here. They’re just enjoying getting to know a ... Klund told Missourinet that Missouri seems to have become a hotspot for Christmas movies as well. “I would say that tax credit has something to do with it, but I also think that we’re in the Midwest, that’s like relatable people,” said Klund. “These are the people that are actually watching these Christmas movies. I think it just makes sense. I mean, we have such adorable small towns in our state.” Klund said the tax credits showed a great return on investment for the state in 2025. “That’s 54 projects over the course of one year. So, we were very close to allocating the full cap. It’s 16 million that we have total. I do think in 2026, we will allocate all of our funding,” said Klund. In 2023, the Missouri Legislature passed and then-Gov. Mike Parson signed a bill into law to resurrect the offering of film tax credits. They are set to expire at the end of 2029, and Klund said she’s hopeful lawmakers will extend them. Copyright © 2026 · Missourinet Share this:

headline: Faith Channel Distribution Launches TheaterFlix - Digital Cinema Report
summary_text: Faith Channel Distribution Launches TheaterFlix - Digital Cinema Report -- Faith Channel Distribution has announced the launch of TheaterFlix, a first-of-its-kind subscription platform created exclusively for movie theatres. TheaterFlix makes it easier than ever for exhibitors to access, schedule, and showcase meaningful, values-driven family films audiences are actively seeking. Through a simple subscription model, theatres receive a curated selection of high-quality, faith-based and family-friendly movies designed to inspire, encourage, and bring communities together. With one subscription unlocking dozens of films—and more being added throughout the year, TheaterFlix offers theaters a consistent and reliable source of fresh content without the traditional barriers of complex licensing, reporting requirements, or revenue sharing. “Our mission with TheaterFlix is to bridge the gap between filmmakers and theaters,” said Chip Rossetti from Faith Channel Distribution. “There is a growing demand for uplifting, faith-centered, and family-friendly stories, yet many theatres struggle to access this content consistently. TheaterFlix solves that problem while also providing an additional, much-needed revenue stream for theatres across the country.” Unlike traditional distribution models, TheaterFlix requires no box office reporting and no revenue sharing, allowing theatres to retain 100 percent of ticket sales while expanding their programming with trusted, values-aligned films. This streamlined approach empowers theatres to better serve families, churches, schools, and community groups looking for wholesome entertainment options on the big screen. TheaterFlix is built on the belief that stories matter, and that theatres play a vital role in bringing people together. By providing dependable access to engaging, values-driven films, Faith Channel Distribution aims to strengthen both exhibitors and the filmmakers creating content rooted in Christian and family values. For more information about TheaterFlix or to subscribe, visit www.theaterflix.org.

headline: Cinema United Attends Ohio Goes to the Movies Kickoff | Cinema United
summary_text: Cinema United Attends Ohio Goes to the Movies Kickoff | Cinema United By Todd HalsteadDirector of Government Relations and Independent Theatre Owner Engagement There’s a particular kind of hush that settles over an auditorium right before a film begins. People shift in their seats. Conversations taper off. The lights go down. And in that moment, a crowd becomes a community ready to be carried ... That feeling was at the center of the Ohio Goes to the Movies kickoff at Township Hall in the small village of Milan—birthplace of Thomas Edison, the “Father of Film.” It was a fitting place to launch OGTTM—a first-of-its-kind, statewide celebration of film as part of America 250–Ohio, running for about 250 days from mid-February through October 2026. It will bring hundreds of free screenings of films with an Ohio connection and events to all 88 counties, hosted by local cinemas, libraries, and cultural spaces. It’s also a reminder of Ohio’s place in movie history: Edison’s ... since, Buckeyes have been leaders in making movie magic in front of and behind the camera. OGTTM celebrates that legacy ... While this was the official start, OGTTM has been years in the making. The seed was planted in 2003, when Stephen ... once felt too big became exactly the kind of community-driven initiative a statewide celebration is meant to support. Program Director Molly Kreuzman, the driving, energetic force behind OGTTM, put it best: what people are seeing now is ... Why theaters are central to the whole idea Timed to fall on Thomas Edison’s birthday, the OGTTM kickoff leaned into the state’s film history in a fun way, ... From the stage, Governor Mike DeWine spoke about why moviegoing matters: you walk into a dark theater, and you’re transported by the music, the visuals, the story. A true moviegoer, he said, “there’s nothing that could be more ... A standout moment was welcoming proud Buckeye Beverly D’Angelo for a Q&A with Peter Lawson Jones, actor and former Ohio ... It was a reminder that Ohio’s film story includes not just locations and history, but the talent and voices audiences ... At the kickoff, I met with Sean Denny, Director of Operations for Cleveland Cinemas, which operates the Capitol Theatre, a movie palace that opened in 1921—one of those places that reminds you theaters are cultural landmarks, not just venues. I also connected with Tracey Peyton and Linda Diamond with the Strand Theatre and Cultural Arts Association in Delaware, Ohio. And I spent time with Cinemark’s Ohio team operating theaters across the state. It was great connecting with these Cinema United members united by the same mission: bringing communities together through ... What Ohio Goes to the Movies will do in 2026 The OGTTM events aren’t just film screenings. Many of the events are designed like “mini premieres with red carpet moments, themed concessions, film facts, introductions, and post-film discussions that turn watching a movie into a community night out. Yesterday was the kickoff. Now comes the payoff: turning planning into audiences, and a statewide ... mission of OGTTM: “And as I like to say, we’re looking back, but we’re thinking forward. We are participating in what comes next. This is Ohio’s moment. This is where movies began. This is where movies will continue. And this is where ... Mr. Governor DeWine likes to say, “Come on, Ohio. headline: Filmed in Central Illinois, ‘Moses the Black’ brings new film opportunities to the area - IPM Newsroom
summary_text: Filmed in Central Illinois, ‘Moses the Black’ brings new film opportunities to the area - IPM Newsroom # Filmed in Central Illinois, ‘Moses the Black’ brings new film opportunities to the area - February 4, 2026 - By A. Oishii Basu Tiktok Instagram Facebook-f Dustin Hoke, left, set designer with Flyover Film Studios; Mark Brown, center, director of event services and film at Experience C-U; and Jen Shelby, right, founder and president of Flyover, discuss the film "Moses the Black" at the Savoy 16 theater in Savoy on Sunday, Feb. 1, 2026. ... https://ipmnewsroom.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/MOSESTHEBLACK_CC.mp3 SAVOY — The Savoy 16 lobby buzzed with filmmakers, moviegoers and community members Sunday as “Moses the Black,” a locally grown feature film shot largely in Central Illinois, premiered to a sold-out crowd. The premiere marked a turning point for Central Illinois’ growing film industry, as sources from local studios said productions like “Moses the Black” show the region can support large-scale projects once limited to major cities. Produced by Curtis “50 Cent” Jackson, the film follows the journey of a gang leader returning home from prison to avenge his best friend. He faces a crisis of faith after his grandmother tells him of a saint who was a reformed ... Andrew Stengele is the director of Champaign Movie Makers, an organization focused on creating local low-budget films in Champaign and screening them. He said a large part of why productions are moving away from cities like Los Angeles is because of tax incentives. Instead, they opt for set locations in Georgia, Canada and now, Illinois. Flyover Film Studios, a local film studio in Rantoul, has taken advantage of that tax incentive situation, Stengele said, and built a big production unit out of the old Chanute Air Force space. ... shoot footage mainly in Chicago and overseas. It ended up in Central Illinois after she scouted the area with Brett ... Illinois in general. Projects like “Moses the Black” and “Chilli Finger” starring Bryan Cranston help Central Illinois gain credibility as a film destination. “It can save a production in the millions to come down here,” Shelby said. “I think when people see that we can film ... In comparison, she said Flyover Film Studios has no parking fees. She said permits to close down streets for filming cost around $25 to $100, compared to Chicago’s $250 per day. Stengele said this combination of affordability, tax incentive and versatility of filming locations are bringing more attention to the film industry here. “I would expect in 2026, in 2027, to see more and more big-name movies, or at least movies with big names being made here in town or in the surrounding area,” Stengele said. Cindependent, an organization that supports independent filmmakers in Cincinnati, will assume the theater's operations from a private group that also operates The Esquire Theatre in Cincinnati's Clifton neighborhood and the Kenwood Theatre in Kenwood. Representatives of that group, Theatre Management Corp., could not immediately be reached for comment. ... “We are absolutely delighted to welcome Cindependent to Mariemont,” Mariemont Mayor Bill Brown said in a press release. "Their commitment to film, arts, and community makes them an ideal steward for the Mariemont Theatre.'' Cindependent has been working closely with the volunteer-led Save the Mariemont Theatre Committee to ensure the historic cinema remains locally operated. Cindependent estimated the transition would take about 16 weeks. The Spinnenweber family, which owns the building and land where the Mariemont Theater is located at 6906 Wooster Pike, has also expressed support for the new operators. “Our family has always believed the Mariemont Theatre should remain a place for community and culture,” family members ... Cindependent plans to honor the theater’s long-standing role as a community gathering place while establishing it as a new home for independent film, local voices, and shared cultural experiences, according to the press release. The theater, which will maintain all of its current employees, will broaden the community’s access to independent, international, and locally produced films while continuing to screen mainstream and family-friendly offerings, ... headline: Midwest Exchange joins Kinowerks in building creative connections Reel Chicago News
summary_text: Midwest Exchange joins Kinowerks in building creative connections Reel Chicago News Menu Menu - Reel Indie # The Midwest Exchange joins Kinowerks in building creative connections - January 15, 2026 - By Reel Chicago Chicago’s creative community is about to gain a new kind of gathering space, one built around collaboration, sustainability, and shared stewardship. The Midwest Exchange, presented by Kinowerks and Midwest Film Festival, is launching as a values-driven initiative designed to keep professional creative resources in motion. The concept is simple but powerful: keeping equipment, materials, and knowledge from sitting idle or going to waste, and instead create a trusted environment where filmmakers and creative professionals can connect, exchange, and reinvest in one another. At its core, the Midwest Exchange emphasizes stewardship over ownership, sustainability in practice, professional care, and long-term investment in community. The goal is not just to host an event, but to strengthen the creative ecosystem ... The partnership brings together two organizations with complementary strengths. Midwest Film Festival contributes more than two decades of nonprofit experience and credibility within the filmmaking community. Kinowerks provides purpose-built creative infrastructure, including screening rooms, studios, and thoughtfully designed event space tailored for production and collaboration. The Midwest Exchange is designed for working filmmakers, technicians, producers, directors, cinematographers, editors, post supervisors, theater and audio professionals, as well as emerging creatives committed to long-term careers. ... The inaugural Midwest Exchange will take place February 21, 2026, from 12–4 p.m. at Kinowerks, 5645 North Ravenswood Avenue in Chicago. Organizers estimate the initiative will reach a substantial regional audience, supported by a combined community that includes tens of thousands of subscribers and followers, along with thousands of annual in-person attendees across both organizations’ programming. That built-in network gives the Exchange the potential to extend its impact well beyond a ... Rather than a traditional sponsorship model, involvement is framed as participation. Supporters are encouraged to engage on-site, contribute to authentic storytelling, and align with professional standards and sustainability in action. Support tiers are structured to reinforce access, infrastructure, and long-term creative growth across the Midwest. For more information or to get involved, contact Grover Derupe at grover.derupe@beesonventures.com. --- --- ALSO READ: 31 Candles reaches theaters with Hannah Welever producing --- ##### Share This: ReelChicago.com/ Copyright © 2026. headline: Milwaukee Film paid $1.55 million for Downer Theatre, new deed says
summary_text: Milwaukee Film paid $1.55 million for Downer Theatre, new deed saysSkip to main content - USA 250 - Shopping - Contributor Content - Staff Directory - For Subscribers - Connect With Us - Give Feedback - Newsletters - Crosswords - My Account - Ideas Lab - Travel - Life - Entertainment - Legals - eNewspaper - Obituaries - Nation / World - Projects and Investigations - Suburbs - Business - Packers - Sports - News - Home # Milwaukee Film paid $1.55 million for Downer Theatre, new deed says --- ## Sale includes new condo filing Milwaukee Journal Sentinel Milwaukee Film Inc. paid $1.55 million in its recent Downer Theatre purchase. That's according to a newly filed deed. The nonprofit group's announcement that it bought Milwaukee's oldest cinema didn't include a purchase price. The theater, 2589 N. Downer Ave., was sold by Seattle-based B33 Downer Avenue LLC to IFM Downer LLC, a Milwaukee Film affiliate, according to a deed posted online by the Wisconsin Department of Revenue on March 4. Milwaukee Film said it was able to purchase the theatre thanks to private donations and a low-interest loan. Bridge33 had owned it since early 2020. Milwaukee Film built on that when it took over the space, and in 2025, the Downer Theatre generated $952,500 for the ... “We have ambitious plans to restore this historic cinema in a way that honors its legacy while ensuring it serves today’s audiences,” said Kerns in part. “Our vision includes upgraded seating, expanded accessibility features, upgraded audio and projection systems, and the addition of 35mm film capabilities to reconnect the theater with its rich tradition of cinema exhibition. We are also planning significant improvements to restrooms and an expanded lobby that will create a more welcoming and inclusive gathering space for our community.” The 2026 Milwaukee Film Festival will take place at the venue, and others, from April 16-30. --- #### Related Stories ##### Downer Theatre reopens for Milwaukee Film FestivalMilwaukee’s oldest movie theater, Downer Theatre, closes unexpectedly ---

(Courtesy photo) Shirley Sneve ICT FARGO — The Northern Plains Indigenous Film Festival debuts April 17 at the historic Fargo Theatre in Fargo, North Dakota, establishing a new home for Indigenous cinema in the Northern Plains and greater Midwest. Founded to create a platform for Indigenous storytelling through film, the festival centers contemporary Indigenous stories while bringing filmmakers, audiences, and creative professionals into the same room. The two-day event will feature Indigenous-made narrative, documentary, and short films, filmmaker conversations, and programming designed to ... While Indigenous film festivals exist across the country, few are rooted in the Northern Plains. The festival was created to fill that gap, offering a space where Indigenous filmmakers can share work grounded in the cultures, histories, and lived experiences of this region while also welcoming national and international perspectives. “Film is one of the most powerful tools Indigenous people have for telling our own stories,” said Joseph Williams, Sisseton Wahpeton Oyate, co-founder and festival co- director. “This festival is about visibility, access, and building ... Anna Johnson, Turtle Mountain Band of Chippewa and also festival co-founder and co-director, said it was a natural fit for the event to be held in Fargo. “Native art is part of Fargo’s identity,” she said. “We aren’t a subcategory. We’re ... Screenings and filmmaker panel discussions will be held at the Fargo Theatre while additional programming will be held at North Dakota State University’s Barry Hall including filmmaker workshops led by Ben West and Yancey Burns of Rena Flying Coyote Collective, Jennifer Martell, and a presentation by Liza Black, author of Picturing Indians, focusing on ... Film submissions are being accepted through March 30. More information on the festival can be found at northernplainsfestival.com. headline: Iowa revives film rebate program years after scandal, but independent filmmakers say it falls short
summary_text: Iowa revives film rebate program years after scandal, but independent filmmakers say it falls short CEDAR RAPIDS, Iowa (KCRG) - Iowa is launching a new film rebate program nearly two decades after its last incentive program ended in scandal. The two-year pilot program offers up to 30% back on qualifying expenses. To qualify, productions must come from Iowa studios, carry a budget of at least $1 million, film mostly in the state, and be made available to the public. The Iowa Economic Development Authority said the Renovo Media Group in Clear Lake, Iowa proposed the concept for the new rebate program. It is Iowa’s first major rebate program since the state abruptly ended its previous incentive program in 2009 after millions of dollars were misused. The state said the new program has more safeguards against fraud, including specifying allowable and non-allowable expenses. It also requires a production’s final budget be reviewed by a third-party before approval. Eric Dean Freese, director of the Cedar Rapids Independent Film Festival, said the program represents progress but leaves out Iowa’s independent filmmakers. “It’s baby steps,” Freese said. He said the new program is not competitive enough with other states. ... appreciates the work that’s being done by those smaller budget productions.” The new rebate program comes as the state recently put its Greenlight Grant for low-budget films on pause — a grant Freese said many filmmakers relied on. The state said it will be evaluating the Greenlight Grant program “to ensure it has met the goals to advance Iowa’s ... Filmmaker Brittany Benedict directed ’ 5683,’ a romantic comedy filmed in Eastern Iowa with a local crew. ... “If you don’t know, that really is not a lot to make a feature film,” Benedict said. “So, we kind of went back to our ... “Maybe just something more on, like, a lower scale, just to really see that there is a lot of talented individuals out ... Actress Caitlin Allen grew up in Cedar Rapids and said it was exciting to film in her hometown. She said the opportunities in Iowa have allowed her to start her career. “It’s a smaller market, but that also allows for really amazing talent to shine through,” Allen said. “I think that that’s really important, especially if you’re going to end up growing your career.” The film is streaming on Amazon Prime. Freese said the film represents Iowa’s growing industry. He said filmmakers just need more resources. “We’re missing out on an opportunity to create jobs, to create work, and to create films that are a calling card to the type of talent and type of work that’s being done in Iowa,” Freese said. The application window for the pilot rebate program is open through March 16, 2026. Funding runs through 2027. In a statement to TV9, the Iowa Economic Development Authority said it hopes the program will support large-scale productions in the state. So far, it says it’s received four applications for the rebate program. #### Most Read #### Latest News

I mean, we have such adorable small towns in our state.” Klund said the tax credits showed a great return on investment for the state in 2025. “That’s 54 projects over the course of one year. So, we were very close to allocating the full cap. It’s 16 million that we have total. I do think in 2026, we will allocate all of our funding,” said Klund. In 2023, the Missouri Legislature passed and then-Gov. Mike Parson signed a bill into law to resurrect the offering of film tax credits. They are set to expire at the end of 2029, and Klund said she’s hopeful lawmakers will extend them. Copyright © 2026 · Missourinet Share this:

headline: Statewide film series celebrates Ohio’s cinema legacy to mark America’s 250th anniversary - cleveland.com
summary_text: Statewide film series celebrates Ohio’s cinema legacy to mark America’s 250th anniversary - cleveland.com Family Sharing is here! Subscribe to Premium Access for $5 # Statewide film series celebrates Ohio’s cinema legacy to mark America’s 250th anniversary - Published: Feb. 10, 2026, 5:24 p.m. - Updated: Feb. 10, 2026, 5:27 p.m. Gov. Mike DeWine and his wife, Fran, will attend the kickoff screening of "Frankenstein" in Milan on Wednesday, launching a roughly 250-day celebration of Ohio's cinema legacy. (John Kuntz, cleveland.com)John Kuntz, cleveland.com MILAN, Ohio — As part of America’s 250th birthday, Ohio is kicking off statewide film series noting the state’s contributions to the silver screen. Gov. Mike DeWine and his wife, Fran, plan to attend the first event in “Ohio Goes to the Movies” on Wednesday in Milan, the birthplace and childhood home of Thomas Edison. The opening event will screen the film “Frankenstein,” produced by Edison’s company. The choice is a nod to Edison, who with his staff developed the Kinetoscope, an early motion picture exhibition device. Edison’s birthday is also on Wednesday. The Ohio Goes to the Movie series will feature films made in Ohio, starring Ohio-born actors, set in Ohio locations, or centered on Ohioans. The series will run from mid-February through October 2026 — approximately 250 days. Ohio also has a rich history of producing leaders in the film industry. Among those are actors Clark Gable, Lillian Gish, Dorothy Dandridge, Doris Day, Paul Newman, Halle Berry, Martin Sheen and Sarah Jessica Parker. Director, producer, and screenwriter Steven Spielberg was born in Cincinnati. Countless films have been shot in Ohio, including, “Shawshank Redemption,” “Deerhunter,” “A Christmas Story,” and “The Avengers.” More recently, “Superman,” released in 2025, was shot in Cleveland. Screenings for the film series will take place in cities and towns across Ohio and include a wide range of genres, from smaller independent films to Hollywood blockbusters. A full program guide can be found online. The kickoff screening begins at 2:30 p.m. Wednesday at Milan Township Hall, 24 Park Street, second floor ballroom. ... Mary Frances McGowan Mary Frances McGowan is a political reporter for Cleveland.com. Prior to joining the team in 2025, McGowan was most recently a staff correspondent at National Journal, where she covered political campaigns for... more [mmcgowan@cleveland.com

All hell breaks loose when she unexpectedly escapes, putting ... Producing are former Sony executive Scott Strauss via his recently-formed banner Badlands, Basil Iwanyk and Erica Lee for Thunder Road, and Johnson, Dany Garcia and Hiram Garcia for Seven Bucks. Gillan is also producing with her partner at Red Riot Pictures, Jess Biddle. Rothstein, Hall, and Warren Goz will executive produce on behalf of North.Five.Six. headline: Filmed in Central Illinois, ‘Moses the Black’ brings new film opportunities to the area - IPM Newsroom
summary_text: Filmed in Central Illinois, ‘Moses the Black’ brings new film opportunities to the area - IPM Newsroom # Filmed in Central Illinois, ‘Moses the Black’ brings new film opportunities to the area - February 4, 2026 - By A. Oishii Basu Tiktok Instagram Facebook-f Dustin Hoke, left, set designer with Flyover Film Studios; Mark Brown, center, director of event services and film at Experience C-U; and Jen Shelby, right, founder and president of Flyover, discuss the film "Moses the Black" at the Savoy 16 theater in Savoy on Sunday, Feb. 1, 2026. ... https://ipmnewsroom.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/MOSESTHEBLACK_CC.mp3 SAVOY — The Savoy 16 lobby buzzed with filmmakers, moviegoers and community members Sunday as “Moses the Black,” a locally grown feature film shot largely in Central Illinois, premiered to a sold-out crowd. The premiere marked a turning point for Central Illinois’ growing film industry, as sources from local studios said productions like “Moses the Black” show the region can support large-scale projects once limited to major cities. Produced by Curtis “50 Cent” Jackson, the film follows the journey of a gang leader returning home from prison to avenge his best friend. He faces a crisis of faith after his grandmother tells him of a saint who was a reformed ... Andrew Stengele is the director of Champaign Movie Makers, an organization focused on creating local low-budget films in Champaign and screening them. He said a large part of why productions are moving away from cities like Los Angeles is because of tax incentives. Instead, they opt for set locations in Georgia, Canada and now, Illinois. Flyover Film Studios, a local film studio in Rantoul, has taken advantage of that tax incentive situation, Stengele said, and built a big production unit out of the old Chanute Air Force space. ... Illinois in general. Projects like “Moses the Black” and “Chilli Finger” starring Bryan Cranston help Central Illinois gain credibility as a film destination. “It can save a production in the millions to come down here,” Shelby said. “I think when people see that we can film with that many locations, and the look is so good… I think it’s really gonna open the door for more and more ... In comparison, she said Flyover Film Studios has no parking fees. She said permits to close down streets for filming cost around $25 to $100, compared to Chicago’s $250 per day. Stengele said this combination of affordability, tax incentive and versatility of filming locations are bringing more attention to the film industry here. “I would expect in 2026, in 2027, to see more and more big-name movies, or at least movies with big names being made here in town or in the surrounding area,” Stengele said. Cindependent, an organization that supports independent filmmakers in Cincinnati, will assume the theater's operations from a private group that also operates The Esquire Theatre in Cincinnati's Clifton neighborhood and the Kenwood Theatre in Kenwood. Representatives of that group, Theatre Management Corp., could not immediately be reached for comment. ... “We are absolutely delighted to welcome Cindependent to Mariemont,” Mariemont Mayor Bill Brown said in a press release. "Their commitment to film, arts, and community makes them an ideal steward for the Mariemont Theatre.'' Cindependent has been working closely with the volunteer-led Save the Mariemont Theatre Committee to ensure the historic cinema remains locally operated. Cindependent estimated the transition would take about 16 weeks. The Spinnenweber family, which owns the building and land where the Mariemont Theater is located at 6906 Wooster Pike, has also expressed support for the new operators. “Our family has always believed the Mariemont Theatre should remain a place for community and culture,” family members ... Cindependent plans to honor the theater’s long-standing role as a community gathering place while establishing it as a new home for independent film, local voices, and shared cultural experiences, according to the press release. The theater, which will maintain all of its current employees, will broaden the community’s access to independent, international, and locally produced films while continuing to screen mainstream and family-friendly offerings, ... headline: Midwest Exchange joins Kinowerks in building creative connections Reel Chicago News
summary_text: Midwest Exchange joins Kinowerks in building creative connections Reel Chicago News Menu Menu - Reel Indie # The Midwest Exchange joins Kinowerks in building creative connections - January 15, 2026 - By Reel Chicago Chicago’s creative community is about to gain a new kind of gathering space, one built around collaboration, sustainability, and shared stewardship. The Midwest Exchange, presented by Kinowerks and Midwest Film Festival, is launching as a values-driven initiative designed to keep professional creative resources in motion. The concept is simple but powerful: keeping equipment, materials, and knowledge from sitting idle or going to waste, and instead create a trusted environment where filmmakers and creative professionals can connect, exchange, and reinvest in one another. At its core, the Midwest Exchange emphasizes stewardship over ownership, sustainability in practice, professional care, and long-term investment in community. The goal is not just to host an event, but to strengthen the creative ecosystem itself. The partnership brings together two organizations with complementary strengths. Midwest Film Festival contributes more than two decades of nonprofit experience and credibility within the filmmaking community. Kinowerks provides purpose-built creative infrastructure, including screening rooms, studios, and thoughtfully designed event space tailored for production and collaboration. The Midwest Exchange is designed for working filmmakers, technicians, producers, directors, cinematographers, editors, post supervisors, theater and audio professionals, as well as emerging creatives committed to long-term careers. ... The inaugural Midwest Exchange will take place February 21, 2026, from 12–4 p.m. at Kinowerks, 5645 North Ravenswood Avenue in Chicago. Organizers estimate the initiative will reach a substantial regional audience, supported by a combined community that includes tens of thousands of subscribers and followers, along with thousands of annual in-person attendees across both organizations’ programming. That built-in network gives the Exchange the potential to extend its impact well beyond a ... Rather than a traditional sponsorship model, involvement is framed as participation. Supporters are encouraged to engage on-site, contribute to authentic storytelling, and align with professional standards and sustainability in action. Support tiers are structured to reinforce access, infrastructure, and long-term creative growth across the Midwest. For more information or to get involved, contact Grover Derupe at grover.derupe@beesonventures.com. --- --- ALSO READ: 31 Candles reaches theaters with Hannah Welever producing --- ##### Share This: ReelChicago.com/ Copyright © 2026. headline: Illinois strengthens film incentives as Gov. Pritzker signs SB 1911 Reel Chicago News
summary_text: Illinois strengthens film incentives as Gov. Pritzker signs SB 1911 Reel Chicago News Menu Menu - PRODUCTION CENTRAL # Illinois strengthens film incentives as Gov. Pritzker signs SB 1911 - December 12, 2025 - By Barbara Roche Illinois filmmakers received a major boost today as Governor JB Pritzker signed SB 1911, a comprehensive update to the Illinois Film Production Services Tax Credit Act. The legislation, which passed during the General Assembly’s fall veto session, modernizes the state’s incentive structure, expands eligibility, and reinforces the industry’s long-term stability through 2039. The signing concludes a months-long push by industry advocates, including the Illinois Production Alliance (IPA), and solidifies Illinois’ position as one of the most competitive production environments in the United States. ### Legislative Timeline and Vote Outcome ... Gov. Pritzker signed SB 1911 into law on December 12, 2025, enacting the most significant upgrade to the film tax credit in more than a decade. The strong vote margins reflect a bipartisan acknowledgement of the film industry’s statewide economic impact, from Chicago’s major studio infrastructure to the rapidly growing production activity in downstate and rural communities. ### Key Enhancements for Illinois Filmmakers SB 1911 implements several changes designed to reduce production costs, strengthen local hiring, and give the Illinois ... - 30% credit on non-resident wages, up to $500,000 per worker, with expanded eligibility for up to 13 non-resident ... - 15% economic-disadvantaged worker bonus, encouraging hiring in underserved communities ... headquartered in Illinois, aligning transportation spending with the state’s goal of keeping more production dollars in-state. ... The program’s extension through 2039 offers filmmakers, studios, and streaming partners the long-range certainty needed ... Illinois has experienced significant production growth in recent years, generating more than $650 million in film and television spending in 2024 and tens of thousands of hires across crew, talent, and support sectors. The updated credit positions the state to retain and expand that momentum. For independent filmmakers, the bonus credits and broader expense categories make regional shoots and smaller-budget projects more attainable. For larger productions, including returning network series and streaming originals, the ... ### Looking Ahead With SB 1911 now law, Illinois enters 2026 with one of the strongest production incentive packages in the country. The enhancements reflect the industry’s evolving needs and demonstrate the state’s commitment to growing its film, television, and commercial sectors. --- ALSO READ: Golden Globe taps Chicago productions with 4 nominations --- ##### Share This: ReelChicago.com/ Copyright © 2026. summary_text: By David Sims Great cinema has never died, but there’s something particularly heartening about the fact that it survived 2025. Looking back at this turbulent year, rife with the usual industry concerns over the viability of the theatrical experience, young people’s slipping attention spans, and Hollywood’s overreliance on franchises, unearths a diverse crop of gems. Many of my favorite films were major studio releases—blockbusters, even—that challenged audiences in innovative, surprising ways. While there were indie triumphs and international standouts to consider, too, this year demonstrated that movie stars can still have cultural impact, and that large budgets can be spent on more than just costumed do-gooders. headline: Tata Packaging Reports Record Q4 Profits Amid Rising Demand for Sustainable Boxes
summary_text: Tata Packaging, a leading Indian supplier of corrugated packaging boxes, announced a 25% year-on-year increase in net profit for Q4 FY2026, driven by strong demand in e-commerce and FMCG sectors. The company is investing ₹500 crore in expanding production capacity for eco-friendly boxes, which could impact pricing and supply reliability for procurement strategies.

headline: Uflex Partners with Global Giant for Innovative Recyclable Packaging Solutions
summary_text: Uflex Ltd., India's largest flexible packaging firm with a strong boxes division, has entered a strategic partnership with a European firm to develop fully recyclable rigid boxes. This collaboration aims to meet new regulatory standards on plastic waste, reducing supplier risk for buyers focusing on sustainability in negotiations.

headline: Packman Industries Faces Regulatory Scrutiny Over Environmental Compliance
summary_text: Packman Industries, a major supplier of folding cartons and boxes in North India, is under investigation by the Central Pollution Control Board for non-compliance with waste management norms. This poses significant supplier risk, potentially leading to production halts and supply disruptions for procurement managers.

headline: JK Paper Launches AI-Enabled Custom Packaging Boxes for E-Commerce
summary_text: JK Paper, prominent in the packaging boxes segment, unveiled an AI-driven platform for personalized, high-strength boxes tailored for e-commerce logistics. The innovation promises 20% cost savings and faster delivery, offering leverage points in supplier negotiations for volume buyers.

headline: Shree Ajit Pulp Secures ₹300 Crore Funding for Box Manufacturing Expansion
summary_text: Shree Ajit Pulp and Paper Ltd., a key supplier of industrial packaging boxes, raised ₹300 crore through a mix of debt and equity to double its production capacity in Gujarat. The funding supports product innovations in biodegradable options, strengthening its position amid rising raw material costs.

Commenting on the results, Ashok Chaturvedi, Chairman and Managing Director, UFlex Group, said: “The packaging industry remains on a strong growth path, supported by rising consumption, GST rationalisation, and an expanding organised retail sector, alongside a growing shift toward value-added and sustainable packaging solutions. Extended producer responsibility (EPR) regulations continue to play a key role in accelerating the industry’s transition toward sustainability across food, pharmaceuticals, FMCG, and consumer sectors." He added: "The recently presented Union Budget 2026–27, which focused on manufacturing, infrastructure, logistics, and exports, is expected to strengthen India’s packaging sector and will reinforce the country’s position as a global manufacturing hub. "With operations spanning across India, Europe, Africa, the Middle East, and the Americas, UFlex continues to ensure ... headline: Aero Packaging procures die-cutting
summary_text: Aero Packaging procures die-cutting -- Switch language:.translate -- The MHC-1300F is intended for cutting and creasing applications in corrugated box converting. Credit: MarcoPace68 / Shutterstock.com. India-based corrugated packaging solutions provider Aero Packaging has procured a die-cutting system from Robus India. The company has selected the Confidence Series MHC-1300F, a heavy-duty automated die-cutting machine. The MHC-1300F is intended for cutting and creasing applications in corrugated box converting. The device includes servo-based control, vision systems, and IoT-enabled functions to support process monitoring and ... In this model name, the “F” denotes an advanced automatic feeding arrangement. According to the stated specifications, the machine operates at up to 5,500 sheets per hour and can process sheet sizes up to 1290mm × 940mm. It is designed to handle corrugated boards up to 8.5mm thick and a range of corrugated paper grades. Robus India noted that its installation base is expanding across India, with Aero Packaging among the newer customers. Aero Packaging produces finished packaging items using a mix of modern machinery. Aero Packaging official Rajat Sharma said: “Being a trusted partner for corrugated cartons, we have built up the trust of customers with our commitment and the fleet of capable machines which we currently run.” “Cutting-edge machines and technologies in our manufacturing facility have been chosen wisely. For instance, the newly installed Confidence Series MHC-1300F die-cutting machine from Robus India is ideally a ‘value for money’ system for corrugated carton production with unsurpassed automation, speed, precision, and efficiency. “We always stay at the forefront of innovation with the constant adoption of such modern and capable machines.” -- ### Sign up for our daily news round-up! ### Give your business an edge with our leading industry insights. headline: West Coast Paper Mills Secures Rs 200 Crore Debt Funding for Expansion Amid Rising Costs
summary_text: West Coast Paper Mills (WCPM), prominent in kraft paper and corrugated boxes, raised Rs 200 crore through NCDs to fund capacity expansion and raw material procurement amid volatile pulp prices. The funding improves liquidity but increases debt burden. Negotiation angle: Leverage supplier's financial strengthening for better pricing in volume deals.

headline: TCL Packaging Faces Regulatory Scrutiny Over Environmental Compliance in Corrugated Production
summary_text: TCL Packaging, a major supplier of packaging boxes in Tamil Nadu, has been issued a notice by the state pollution control board for non-compliance with wastewater discharge norms in its manufacturing units. The company risks fines up to Rs 10 crore and production halts. Risk management note: Assess supplier's compliance status; diversify sourcing to mitigate disruptions.

headline: Uflex Ltd Announces Rs 500 Crore Investment in New Corrugated Box Manufacturing Facility
summary_text: Uflex, India's leading flexible packaging company expanding into rigid packaging, has announced a Rs 500 crore investment to set up a state-of-the-art corrugated packaging boxes plant in Uttar Pradesh. This move aims to boost production capacity by 30% and introduce sustainable, recyclable box innovations amid rising demand from e-commerce and FMCG sectors. Key for procurement: potential for cost-competitive supplies and reduced lead times.

headline: Shree Ajit Pulp and Paper Innovates Biodegradable Packaging Boxes for E-Commerce
summary_text: Shree Ajit Pulp and Paper launched a new line of 100% biodegradable corrugated boxes using agricultural waste fibers, compliant with upcoming plastic waste regulations. Initial rollout in Maharashtra with pilots showing 20% cost savings. Strategic value: Aligns with sustainability goals in procurement strategies.

headline: Regulatory Update: New BIS Standards for Corrugated Packaging Boxes Effective April 2026
summary_text: Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) has mandated stricter strength and recyclability norms for all packaging boxes used in food and pharma sectors, impacting suppliers like Uflex and JK Paper. Non-compliant products face market bans. Key for strategy: Audit suppliers for certification to avoid disruptions.

headline: JK Paper Partners with Global Tech Firm for Smart Packaging Boxes
summary_text: JK Paper Ltd has entered a strategic partnership with a US-based IoT company to develop smart corrugated boxes with embedded sensors for real-time tracking and temperature control. The collaboration targets pharma and food industries, enhancing supply chain visibility. Procurement implications: innovation in product offerings, potential premium pricing, but opportunities for long-term contracts.

headline: Mondi Group Acquires Indian Packaging Boxes Firm PackSavvy Solutions
summary_text: Global packaging giant Mondi has acquired 51% stake in PackSavvy Solutions, a Hyderabad-based corrugated boxes manufacturer, for Rs 350 crore. The deal enhances Mondi's India footprint and brings advanced folding box technologies. Procurement insight: Watch for supply chain integration risks and potential pricing pressures post-acquisition.

summary_text: UFlex delivers resilient Q3; EBITDA jumps sequentially as macro tailwinds build ### UFlex delivers resilient Q3; EBITDA jumps sequentially as macro tailwinds build By: ICN Bureau Last updated : February 15, 2026 8:56 am The improvement came even as sales volumes declined 3.7% year-on-year to 151,245 MT Flexible packaging major UFlex has reported a resilient third quarter for FY26, posting strong sequential profitability gains despite pricing pressure, tariff uncertainty and GST-related disruption. Consolidated net revenue for Q3 FY26 stood at Rs. 3,632.9 crore, while normalized EBITDA rose to Rs. 4,39.5 crore, delivering a 12.1% margin — up 200 basis points quarter-on-quarter. Net profit came in at Rs. 48.6 crore. The improvement came even as sales volumes declined 3.7% year-on-year to 151,245 MT, reflecting softer realizations and ... For the first nine months of FY26, revenue edged up 0.8% YoY to Rs. 11,415.7 crore, while volumes remained stable at 482,910 MT (+0.1% YoY). Net profit rebounded sharply to Rs. 121.1 crore, compared to a net loss of Rs. 26.2 crore in the same period last year — underscoring operational resilience amid macro headwinds and supply chain disruptions. Commenting on the results, Ashok Chaturvedi, Chairman and Managing Director, UFlex Group, said: “The packaging industry remains on a strong growth path, supported by rising consumption, GST rationalisation, and an expanding organised retail sector, alongside a growing shift toward value-added and sustainable packaging solutions. Extended producer responsibility (EPR) regulations continue to play a key role in accelerating the industry’s transition toward sustainability across food, pharmaceuticals, FMCG, and consumer sectors." He added: "The recently presented Union Budget 2026–27, which focused on manufacturing, infrastructure, logistics, and exports, is expected to strengthen India’s packaging sector and will reinforce the country’s position as a global manufacturing hub. Anantshree Chaturvedi, Vice Chairman & CEO, Flex Films International, added: “The outlook for the packaging industry remains strong. "With operations spanning across India, Europe, Africa, the Middle East, and the Americas, UFlex continues to ensure ... With a diversified geographic mix — 56% international revenue and 44% domestic — UFlex is positioning itself to benefit ... Mexico. headline: Aero Packaging procures die-cutting
summary_text: Aero Packaging procures die-cutting -- Switch language:.translate -- The MHC-1300F is intended for cutting and creasing applications in corrugated box converting. Credit: MarcoPace68 / Shutterstock.com. India-based corrugated packaging solutions provider Aero Packaging has procured a die-cutting system from Robus India. The company has selected the Confidence Series MHC-1300F, a heavy-duty automated die-cutting machine. The MHC-1300F is intended for cutting and creasing applications in corrugated box converting. The device includes servo-based control, vision systems, and IoT-enabled functions to support process monitoring and ... In this model name, the “F” denotes an advanced automatic feeding arrangement. According to the stated specifications, the machine operates at up to 5,500 sheets per hour and can process sheet sizes up to 1290mm × 940mm. It is designed to handle corrugated boards up to 8.5mm thick and a range of corrugated paper grades. Robus India noted that its installation base is expanding across India, with Aero Packaging among the newer customers. Aero Packaging produces finished packaging items using a mix of modern machinery. Aero Packaging official Rajat Sharma said: “Being a trusted partner for corrugated cartons, we have built up the trust of customers with our commitment and the fleet of capable machines which we currently run.” “Cutting-edge machines and technologies in our manufacturing facility have been chosen wisely. For instance, the newly installed Confidence Series MHC-1300F die-cutting machine from Robus India is ideally a ‘value for money’ system for corrugated carton production with unsurpassed automation, speed, precision, and efficiency. “We always stay at the forefront of innovation with the constant adoption of such modern and capable machines.” -- ### Sign up for our daily news round-up! ### Give your business an edge with our leading industry insights. headline: Europa Unveils New Strategy to Lead UK Logistics
summary_text: Europa Unveils New Strategy to Lead UK Logistics 05 Mar 2026 More in ###### Logistics ###### Materials Handling / Logistics # Europa Worldwide Group Launches New UK Logistics Strategy News 2 mins read Ten years after the Brexit referendum, Europa Worldwide Group (EWG) has outlined a new strategy to strengthen its position as the UK’s leading logistics provider for goods moving between the UK and the EU. Creds: Europa Marking its 60th anniversary, the independent logistics company has introduced a refreshed brand identity and manifesto, “Powered by Better”, signalling the start of a new phase of expansion following more than a decade of rapid ... Europa, which has quadrupled in size over the past 13 years and significantly expanded its Air & Sea freight and 3PL warehousing operations, unveiled its new strategy at an event on 3 March at its Kent headquarters. Industry leaders, ... ## A Model for Frictionless Trade Since post-Brexit trade rules came into effect in 2020, Europa’s road freight division has transported twice the volume of goods handled by its nearest competitor, moving £11.2bn worth of products between the UK and the EU. This performance is driven by Europa Flow, the company’s “frictionless” delivery solution powered by proprietary software that removes customs delays for both SMEs and multinational customers. Europa Flow has been recognised as a new model for logistics providers navigating UK–EU trade, with customers crediting ... “The last six years have been a litmus test for the UK logistics sector,” said Andrew Baxter, CEO and Owner of Europa ... “‘Powered by Better’ perfectly communicates our DNA,” Baxter said. “In logistics, you cannot settle for second best. We work relentlessly to find the marginal gains that give our customers a market-leading edge. As we celebrate 60 years since our founding in 1966, we aren't just looking back at our heritage—we are asserting our role as the company-of-choice for the next generation of global traders.” ... From its origins as the first express service provider to Europe, Europa now employs 1,300 people across 30 global sites and operates in 160 countries. Its portfolio includes: Europa Road: Offering a Money Back Guarantee on European freight. Europa Warehouse: Managing more than one million sq. ft. of automated and manual 3PL fulfilment space across Corby, Dartford and Birmingham. Europa Air & Sea: Expanding rapidly with hubs in Hong Kong, China, the UAE, India and the UK. With dedicated customs specialists, bonded warehousing and global air and sea supply chain capabilities, Europa is ... “We have 60 years of experience as our foundation and a family of employees committed to delivering for customers no matter the challenge that confronts them,” Baxter added. “Whether it’s moving goods across the Channel Tunnel or the South China Sea, we are ready for the next 60 years. We are Powered by Better.” ## Comments ## Related Articles

headline: Eurovignette: Council clarifies and simplifies EU rules on road tolls and user charges for heavy duty vehicles - Consilium
summary_text: Eurovignette: Council clarifies and simplifies EU rules on road tolls and user charges for heavy duty vehicles - Consilium Skip to content - 4 March 2026 11:17 - Press release - Council of the EU # Eurovignette: Council clarifies and simplifies EU rules on road tolls and user charges for heavy duty vehicles This press release was updated on 5 March 2026 to include the link to the mandate for negotiations with the European Parliament. Today the Council agreed on a negotiation mandate for targeted amendments to the Eurovignette directive, setting the rules for road tolls and user charges for heavy duty vehicles (HDVs) in the EU. The proposed changes focus on improving the legal clarity of the current directive, simplifying its implementation and ensuring the consistent application of CO₂-based charging rules. ... Considering the forthcoming application of CO₂-based charging rules to the vehicle sub-groups, the Council has adopted a position for rapid action and clarity for both operators and administrations. The set trajectory towards a clear ... The mandate clarifies some key provisions of the current directive. These clarifications are important as new CO2-emission standards for heavy-duty vehicles will impact the directive from 1 July 2026, and their smooth interaction is needed to advance in the calculation of road charges according to CO2 emissions. ## Main elements in the Council position The Council negotiating mandate most importantly: ... - Clarifies definitions related to CO2-emissions, including zero-emission vehicles, low-emission heavy-duty vehicles, ... The Council’s position also acknowledges the climate benefits of certain modifications to a vehicle’s technical characteristics — whether through software updates or physical upgrades — known as retrofitting. However, under the ... ## Trailer-based toll reductions The Council decided not to retain a proposal to reduce road charges for HDVs with more sustainable trailers due to potential additional administrative burden, more complex toll pricing and its potential impact on the introduction of electronic toll services and the need to respect existing concession contracts. Finally, the proposed trailer categories could have resulted in a tolling reduction, even when combined with less modern, more CO2-emitting trucks. ## Next steps ... - Amendment of the original Commission proposal - Mandate for negotiations with the European Parliament ## Press contacts Niels Timmermans Press officer - @nielstimmermans.bsky.social - @timmermansniels - +32 2 281 32 65 - +32 470 18 22 47 If you are not a journalist, please send your request to the public information service. Road transport plays a central role in these supply chains and will need significant support to transition to zero-emission operations. In particular, investments in enabling infrastructure will be essential, including charging and refuelling infrastructure for commercial fleets, depot charging for trucks and buses, and the grid connections ... headline: Europe’s Bet on Non-European Drivers to Keep Goods Moving | Transportational.com
summary_text: Europe’s Bet on Non-European Drivers to Keep Goods Moving | Transportational.com Europe’s transportation market is quietly approaching a structural turning point, one that doesn’t involve new engines, hydrogen corridors, or autonomous convoys, but people—specifically, who gets to sit behind the wheel. The EU is moving toward opening its internal market to non-European truck drivers, a step that acknowledges something logistics operators have been muttering about for years at truck stops and boardrooms alike: the driver shortage is no longer cyclical, it’s demographic and chronic. The European Commission is now preparing to simplify recruitment procedures and establish common rules that protect drivers from abuse while ensuring fair working conditions, effectively pulling this ... where professional drivers are available and willing to work. The countries most often mentioned—Egypt, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Morocco, Tunisia, and Kenya—are not random picks; they are regions with established driving professions, younger workforces, and strong incentives to access stable EU employment. At the request of the European Commission, the International Road Transport Union, known across the industry simply as IRU, prepared a comprehensive report examining what it really takes to employ drivers from outside Europe at scale. The report doesn’t sugarcoat the challenges. Visa processing times remain a bottleneck, professional qualifications earned ... Europe has no realistic path to filling roughly 500,000 open positions in its transport sector. That number isn’t abstract. It shows up as delayed deliveries, rising freight rates, stressed supply chains, and drivers working ... This is where SDM4EU, Skilled Driver Mobility for Europe, enters the picture as more than just another Brussels acronym. The project is designed to translate policy intent into operational reality, developing procedures that allow drivers from third countries to be legally, rapidly, and safely employed across EU Member States. It’s about building a ... shared process. Pilot projects scheduled for the second half of 2026 will be the real test, the moment when theory meets the hard edges of border control, licensing offices, and real trucks needing real drivers on real roads. For the transportation market, the implications are significant. A successful opening of the labor pool could stabilize capacity, ease wage inflation driven purely by scarcity, and reduce the operational risk that has crept into road freight since the pandemic years. At the same time, it raises questions operators can’t ignore: how to integrate ... public trust in safety and labor protections. The Commission’s insistence on common rules and safeguards is not window ... Whether this move becomes a long-term fix or a transitional bridge will depend on execution, not intent. If visa ... little relief. But if SDM4EU delivers and IRU’s recommendations are acted on with urgency, Europe may finally align its transportation policy with demographic reality. Trucks will keep moving, supply chains will breathe a little easier, and the driver’s seat—once an overlooked pressure point—may become a symbol of how global labor mobility reshapes regional markets, one kilometer at a time. Save settings Accept All Reject All -- # Europe’s road haulage recovery isn’t catching up Europe’s road haulage sector is recovering, but the pace remains slow and uneven. Transport demand is edging up after the downturn of 2023, yet volumes are still below pre-crisis levels. According to an analysis by ING, European road transport demand is expected to grow by around 1% in 2026, with large differences between countries, freight segments and fleet profiles. Transport demand improved in 2025 following the sharp contraction two years earlier. However, total transport volumes measured in tonne-kilometres remain on average around 1.5% below earlier peaks. ING expects moderate and stable growth this year, but stresses that the recovery remains fragile and uneven across Europe. Protectionist trade policies, geopolitical instability and their knock-on effects on supply chains and consumer confidence are set to continue shaping the market in 2026. Industrial goods remain the weakest segment for road haulage. Energy-intensive industries, including steel and chemicals, continue to struggle with competitiveness, leading to production cuts and site closures across several European countries. Germany, which accounts for more than a quarter of European road transport activity, remains a particular drag on overall volumes. Truck mileage data from Germany point to stagnating volumes, while countries such as Spain and Poland are recording stronger growth in line with more robust economic performance. Hauliers across Europe reduced capacity following the demand contraction of 2023. According to ING, the European ... and the removal of older vehicles from service. Despite some improvement in freight rates, margins remain under pressure. Spot rates, which account for roughly one-fifth of the market, converged with contract rates after the 2023 downturn, underlining the sector’s underlying weakness. Rising wage costs and higher toll charges, particularly in countries such as the Netherlands and Belgium, will be difficult to pass on fully in 2026. ING expects consolidation to continue as smaller companies face increasing pressure from investment requirements related to digitalisation, sustainability and regulatory compliance.
